symbols: stone water jars — the vessels of Jewish purification transformed by Christ into vessels of new wine
abundant wine — the overflowing grace of the new covenant surpassing the old
wedding banquet — the messianic feast and the joy of the kingdom of God
bride and groom — the covenant relationship between Christ and his people
Jesus blessing the water — divine power transforming the ordinary into the extraordinary
description: A large, richly appointed banquet scene showing a long table crowded with guests. At center, Jesus sits among the wedding party, gesturing with one hand. A bride in an ornate headdress sits nearby. The table is laden with food and dishes. In the foreground, large stone water jars are visible on the floor, and a servant tends to them. Musicians play at the right. Golden draperies hang as a canopy above the table. The scene is warm and festive, with many figures in colorful garments engaged in conversation and celebration.
theological_description: Cossiers's Wedding at Cana depicts Christ's first public miracle as recorded in John 2:1-11. When the wine ran out at a wedding feast in Cana of Galilee, Mary told Jesus, 'They have no wine.' Jesus directed the servants to fill six stone water jars — 'each holding twenty or thirty gallons' — then said, 'Now draw some out and take it to the master of the feast.' When the master tasted the water that had become wine, he marveled: 'You have kept the good wine until now' (John 2:3-10). John concludes: 'This, the first of his signs, Jesus did at Cana in Galilee, and manifested his glory. And his disciples believed in him' (John 2:11). The miracle operates on multiple theological levels. The transformation of water into wine signifies the new covenant surpassing the old — the water of Jewish ritual purification becomes the wine of messianic joy. The wedding setting itself is prophetic: Isaiah foresaw the messianic age as a feast of 'well-aged wine' (Isaiah 25:6), and Jesus repeatedly compared the kingdom to a wedding banquet (Matthew 22:1-14). The superabundance — some 120 gallons of the finest wine — reveals the lavish, overflowing nature of divine grace. This first sign points forward to the Last Supper where wine would become Christ's own blood of the new covenant (Matthew 26:28).

scripture_refs.reason: The Wedding at Cana — Jesus turning water into wine, his first miracle manifesting his glory
The messianic feast of well-aged wine — the abundance of wine at Cana as a sign of the kingdom
The kingdom of heaven compared to a wedding banquet — the wedding setting as eschatological sign
The wine of the new covenant in Christ's blood — Cana's wine foreshadowing the Eucharistic wine
This first sign manifesting his glory and his disciples believing — the revelatory purpose of the miracle

_meta.title: The Wedding at Cana, Jesus Blesses the Water
